name: How much extra does custom sizing add to door installation?

mainEntity.name: What happens to price and timeline when a door must be cut down or ordered to a nonstandard size?
mainEntity.text: What happens to price and timeline when a door must be cut down or ordered to a nonstandard size?

acceptedAnswer.text: Custom sizing often adds cost because it reduces the chance of a quick, standard install. If the opening is nonstandard, the contractor may need to order a special-size door, modify the frame, or perform additional carpentry so the new unit fits correctly. This can also extend the timeline because custom doors may take longer to manufacture or deliver. Even when a door can be trimmed or adjusted, the installer still has to verify that the final fit is tight enough to seal properly and operate smoothly. In older homes, custom sizing may be the only way to avoid a sloppy fit. Before approving the work, ask whether the door will be made to order, whether field modifications are expected, and whether the quote includes the extra labor needed to make a nonstandard opening look and function like a standard one.
acceptedAnswer.description: Custom sizing usually increases both cost and lead time because the door may need special ordering, extra carpentry, or finishing work. Nonstandard openings can also require frame adjustments. The more the project deviates from a standard replacement, the more likely it is to involve extra labor and delays.
